system.description:
The Ashfoot are the working-class soldiers of Tesshari — the people for whom combat is not a profession of honor but a condition of survival. They are not selected for martial training. They do not serve lords by formal oath. They are not born into the Caste of Steel's upper ranks. They are the people who live in the Broken Reaches' lower territories, who exist in the margins between the warlords' formal armies, and who have learned to fight because the alternative was to die without putting up a struggle. They are militia fighters, common soldiers, Hollow Poor warriors, resistance members, and village defenders. The name comes from a Tesshari slang term for the lowest-caste soldiers — the ones who march through ash and mud while the Ironclad ride at the front. Ashfoot took the name back. Most things the nobility uses as an insult end up becoming something else when the people it's aimed at decide to own it.

An Ashfoot does not have access to quality forge work. Their enhancements are whatever they could afford, salvage, or receive from a sympathetic Forge Tender — which means they are mismatched, partially integrated, held together with substitute resonant alloys, and frequently not precisely calibrated. This is, in practical terms, a significant disadvantage. But Ashfoot have been compensating for disadvantage their entire lives, and they have developed an approach to combat that treats improvisation not as a workaround but as a core method. An Ironclad uses the right tool for each situation because they have the right tools. An Ashfoot uses whatever is at hand, and they have become supernaturally good at making whatever is at hand sufficient.

What the Ashfoot know about combat that the nobility does not: it is mostly waiting and fear, punctuated by moments of appalling violence that nobody on either side is truly prepared for. The Ironclad's kata system was built to manage this reality by converting it into form — turn the chaos into discipline, the fear into procedure. The Ashfoot manage the same reality by going through it rather than over it. They know that fear does not go away if you name it correctly. They know that the right attack is the one that hits, not the one that looks correct. They know that the soldier who survives is often not the best-trained one but the one who stayed calm when everything went sideways and found a way to make the worst moment slightly less final.

The Ashfoot in the Broken Reaches are both essential and overlooked — the second category because of the first. No lord's army functions without common soldiers. No territory holds without the people who live there being willing to defend it. But the Caste hierarchy does not count Ashfoot in the ledgers of military honor. Their deaths are tallied in unit numbers, not names. Their victories are attributed to the samurai who commanded them. The Iron Sutra's theology allows for the Echominds of low-caste soldiers to reach the Iron Afterlife — it does not suggest that those Echominds deserve the same quality of resonant passage. The Ashfoot know what they are worth to the system. Some of them have decided to be worth more than that anyway.
